title: Thinking Machines releases Inkling, a 975B open-weights multimodal MoE with 41B active parameters
subtitle: Mira Murati's lab shipped its first model trained from scratch — text, image, audio, and video, tunable on the lab's Tinker platform, and priced at zero because the weights are public.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Thinking Machines Lab — the AI startup founded by former OpenAI CTO Mira Murati — released Inkling on July 15, a 975-billion-parameter open-weights Mixture-of-Experts model with 41 billion active parameters. It was pretrained on roughly 45 trillion tokens of text, images, audio, and video, supports a 1M-token context window, and includes a controllable 'thinking effort' knob that lets a caller trade tokens for reasoning depth. A lighter preview called Inkling-Small ships alongside it with 12B active parameters, and both are fine-tunable on the lab's Tinker platform.

The technical facts matter less than the framing the lab chose. Thinking Machines is not claiming Inkling is the strongest model available — it explicitly is not. Instead, the pitch is that a customizable, multimodal, open-weights base is more useful to the median builder than a closed leaderboard-topper they can only rent. That is a different theory of value than OpenAI's or Anthropic's, and it comes from an ex-OpenAI CTO with the capital and the reputation to make it stick. The competitor Inkling most directly pressures is not GPT-5.6 Sol or Claude Sonnet 5, but the pricing floor of every closed frontier API.

The release lands in a market that has been drifting toward open weights all year. Meituan's LongCat 2 and Alibaba's Qwen series have kept Chinese open-weight releases in the frontier conversation, Meta's Muse Spark 1.1 came out a week ago, and Zhipu's founder used an internal memo last weekend to argue that openness itself is a security posture. Inkling adds an American open-weights base built by an ex-frontier team, which changes what U.S. enterprises can plausibly self-host and fine-tune without a China-provenance conversation.

A note for learners: when you see 'open-weights' in a headline, read the license terms and the trained data disclosure before you form an opinion. Not every open-weights release is open-source in the classical sense — some carry usage restrictions, telemetry requirements, or ambiguity about commercial use. But the practical unlock for a student or early-career engineer is real: you can now download a 975B multimodal model to a workstation with enough VRAM, prod it, break it, and learn from what it does. That was closed-lab-only territory eighteen months ago.
